tax: Placed at genus rank, Worked-Earthkin is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a mixed-chemistry earth body drawing from a clay-limestone contact or organic-rich channel. A new species belongs here only if it shows a mixed-earth body with both mineral and organic components.
ap: The grouping is recognised by a pale crumbling upright figure or a dark slumping mound. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — pale gritty crumbles with a chalk smell, or a dark smear with a humic smell — which survives even when the body disperses.
eco: Worked-Earthkin occupies marl flats, clay-limestone contacts, and organic-rich root channels. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where earth-pressure animates a mixed-chemistry seam or accumulation and recur with the contact or channel replenishes from both source layers, sustaining themselves on earth-pressure in a mixed-chemistry seam.
beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: poses as a natural mound or accumulation, advances to entomb or adhere. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
traitsText: Shared characters: a mixed-earth body, crushing immunity, and a seam-chemical failure. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each species and species beneath this node.
abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: entombing, adhering, and seam-draw repair. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
bg: In the Throat, Worked-Earthkin matters because makes marl flats and organic channels slow-entombment and adhesion traps. It lets survey reports sort mixed-earth hazards by chemistry, so a crew knows whether to expect crumbling mass or sticky slump without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.
g: Worked-Earthkin is the genus-rank grouping of organics-bearing and mixed-chemistry loam and marl elementals within the True Elementals. Its members share a body of worked or mixed earth — marl or loam — containing both mineral and organic chemistry, and divide below into species groups by whether the earth is lime-dominated and pale-stiff (marl) or organics-dominated and dark-soft (loam). These are the elementals of soils and contact zones, the bridge between pure mineral and biological-matrix earth.
